text: Abstract: The cathodic and anodic transfer coefficient α c and α a are quantities, which are extensively used in the kinetic investigation of electrode processes. In the 3 rd edition of the IUPAC Green Book, the cathodic transfer coefficient is defined as: α c = −( RT / nF )( d ln k c /d E ) , where k c is the electroreduction rate constant, E is the applied potential, and R, T , and F have their usual significance. This definition is obviously equivalent to the other, -( RT / nF )( d ln| j c |/d E ), where j c is the cathodic current density corrected for the variations of the reactant concentration at the electrode surface with respect to its bulk value. The anodic transfer coefficient is defined similarly by simply replacing j c with the anodic current density j a and the minus sign with the plus sign. It is easily seen that the above definitions of α c and α a apply to an electrode reaction, which consists of a single elementary step, consisting of the simultaneous transfer to the electrode of n electrons in the case of α c and their simultaneous departure from the electrode in the case of α a . However, in view of the general validity of the absolute rate theory of the electron transfer proposed by Marcus, an elementary step, which implies the simultaneous departure or transfer of more than one electron is highly improbable. The conditions, which have to be fulfilled in order that such an unlikely event could occur, are examined. Moreover, the majority of electrode reactions do not consist of a single elementary step but are multistep, multi-electron processes. Therefore, the uncritical use of the definitions of α c and α a leads to unwarranted mechanistic interpretations of the electrode process under investigation. In fact, the only directly measurable experimental quantity is d ln| j |/d E . A simple dimensional analysis shows that this quantity can be readily made dimensionless upon multiplication by RT / F giving: ( RT / F )( d ln| j |/d E ). A few examples are given to illustrate the common source of misinterpretation, arising from the definition of the transfer coefficient quoted above. The quantity ( RT / F )( d ln| j |/d E ) is set as equal to α n and n is obtained from α n (usually a quantity close to 0.5) using some assumptions about the value of α . A value for n is then obtained, the meaning of which is either that of the number of electrons of the rate determining step of the reaction or that of the overall reaction. A few examples of such arbitrary interpretations are reported. In view of what has been discussed, it is recommended to define the cathodic and anodic transfer coefficients as: α c = −( RT / F )( d ln| j c |/d E ) and α a = ( RT / F )( d ln j a /d E ), which is clearly independent of any mechanistic consideration.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
